content.text: Cloud computing delivers computing resources — servers, storage, databases, networking, software, analytics, and intelligence — over the internet, allowing organizations and individuals to consume IT capabilities on demand without owning or maintaining physical infrastructure. Microsoft Azure is one of the largest cloud platforms in the world, offering more than 200 products spanning every layer of the technology stack, all accessible through a browser, command-line, or API.
What is the Cloud?
The term
"the cloud"
refers to a global network of remote data centers operated by cloud providers that host and run computing resources. When something is described as
"in the cloud,"
it means it runs on shared infrastructure in one or more of these data centers rather than on a device or server that the user owns and operates directly.

Formally, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) defines cloud computing as a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. This definition captures the five essential characteristics that distinguish cloud computing from older hosting models: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service.

In practical terms, the cloud replaces the purchase of servers and the upfront cost of data-center space with a subscription or pay-per-use billing model. A startup can provision a globally distributed database in minutes, scale it to millions of users without buying hardware, and pay only for the storage and requests actually consumed.
Why the Cloud Matters

Before cloud computing became mainstream, every organization that needed IT capability had to buy, provision, configure, and maintain its own hardware. This created a series of well-understood problems.

Capital expenditure was front-loaded and risky. A company building a new application had to estimate peak load years in advance, buy enough servers to handle that load, and accept that those servers would sit largely idle the rest of the time. If the estimate was wrong in either direction — too low and the service degraded under real demand, too high and capital was wasted.

Time to market was measured in months, not hours. Provisioning a server room, installing racks, connecting networking equipment, loading operating systems, and applying security patches all took weeks before a single line of application code could run in production.

Geographic reach required massive investment. Running a low-latency service for users in Asia, Europe, and North America meant maintaining three separate data centers, each with its own staff, hardware refresh cycles, and compliance obligations.

The cloud eliminates or dramatically reduces each of these constraints. A team can deploy globally in hours, scale automatically with demand, and convert capital expenditure into predictable operational expenditure — freeing financial and engineering resources to focus on what differentiates the product rather than on managing infrastructure.
Core Benefits
Elasticity and auto-scaling.

Cloud resources can scale up or down in seconds. A retail platform can automatically provision hundreds of additional servers on Black Friday and release them by Monday, paying only for the hours used.
Pay-as-you-go pricing.
Resources are billed by consumption — per second of CPU time, per gigabyte of storage, per million API calls. There is no minimum commitment for most services, eliminating idle hardware costs.
Global reach.

Azure operates in more than 60 regions worldwide. Deploying to a new geography requires selecting a region in the portal or CLI, not procuring physical equipment. Content delivery networks can serve static assets from edge locations within milliseconds of users everywhere.
Managed services.

Cloud providers operate the underlying infrastructure of many higher-level services. A managed database like Azure SQL Database handles backups, patching, replication, and failover automatically. Teams consume database capability without running a database server.
Innovation velocity.

Hundreds of new services and features are released each year. Teams can adopt AI inference, vector databases, event streaming, or quantum-inspired optimization APIs without standing up any infrastructure.
Reliability and redundancy.

Major cloud platforms offer Service Level Agreements (SLAs) of 99.9% to 99.999% uptime on individual services. Data is replicated across multiple availability zones and sometimes across regions, providing resilience that would be prohibitively expensive to self-build.
Security and compliance.

Cloud providers invest heavily in physical security, network security, and compliance certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, FedRAMP, HIPAA, GDPR). This shared-responsibility model offloads a significant portion of the compliance burden.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Ongoing operational cost.

Pay-as-you-go pricing is efficient at small scale or variable workloads, but a steady, large, predictable workload may cost more in the cloud than on owned hardware after a few years. Reserved instances and savings plans mitigate this.
Vendor lock-in.

Each cloud platform has proprietary services, APIs, and deployment tools. Migrating a workload that uses Azure-specific services (Cosmos DB, Azure Service Bus, Durable Functions) to another provider requires significant re-engineering.
Egress costs.

Inbound data is usually free; outbound data (data leaving the cloud) is charged per gigabyte. Applications that move large data sets out of the cloud — for analytics, backups, or customer delivery — can face surprisingly large egress bills.
Network latency and dependency.

Cloud workloads depend on internet connectivity. Latency to the cloud adds round-trip time for every remote call. Edge computing, local caches, and CDNs can reduce this, but they add architectural complexity.
Compliance and data sovereignty.

Some regulated industries or jurisdictions require data to remain within specific geographic boundaries. Not every cloud region has every compliance certification, and some data cannot legally leave a country's borders.
Shared tenancy.

Most cloud services run on shared infrastructure. Noisy-neighbor effects — where another tenant's workload affects shared hardware — can appear as unpredictable performance. Dedicated hosts and isolated environments are available but cost more.
How Cloud Computing Works
At its foundation, cloud computing is built on three technologies: virtualization, distributed systems, and software-defined networking.
Virtualization

Virtualization allows a single physical server to be split into many isolated virtual machines (VMs), each running its own operating system and workload. A hypervisor — software like Microsoft Hyper-V — sits between the physical hardware and the VMs, allocating CPU cores, RAM, and storage to each guest and enforcing isolation between them. This is what makes multi-tenancy possible: dozens of customers can share the same physical host without seeing each other's data or processes.

Containers extend this model further. Rather than virtualizing an entire machine, containers share the host OS kernel but isolate the filesystem, process tree, and network namespace for each workload. This makes containers far lighter than VMs (milliseconds to start vs. seconds) and is the foundation of Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) and Azure Container Instances.
Distributed Infrastructure and Redundancy

A cloud region is a cluster of one or more data centers in close geographic proximity, connected by high-speed private fiber. Within a region, Azure groups data centers into Availability Zones — physically separate facilities with independent power, cooling, and networking. Deploying across multiple availability zones means a power outage in one data center does not take down the application.

Data stored in Azure is automatically replicated according to the storage redundancy option selected. Locally Redundant Storage (LRS) keeps three copies within a single data center. Zone-Redundant Storage (ZRS) spreads copies across availability zones. Geo-Redundant Storage (GRS) replicates to a paired region hundreds of miles away, protecting against a regional disaster.
Software-Defined Everything

In a traditional data center, networking is configured by physically cabling switches and routers and entering commands on the devices themselves. Cloud networking is entirely software-defined: virtual networks (VNets in Azure), subnets, routing tables, firewalls, and load balancers are created via API calls. This means network topology can be changed or duplicated in seconds, enabling infrastructure-as-code patterns where the entire environment — networking, compute, storage — is declared in a version-controlled configuration file.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the high-level flow of a typical cloud interaction: client devices connect to cloud services through the public internet, and the cloud provider hosts a rich ecosystem of compute, storage, database, networking, AI, and security services running across global data centers.

attrs.alt: Cloud computing architecture diagram showing users connecting via the internet to Azure cloud services including compute, storage, database, networking, AI/ML, and security

content.text: Each service category represents dozens of individual products. Compute ranges from bare-metal virtual machines with up to 96 vCPUs to serverless functions that execute in under a millisecond and charge by the invocation. Storage spans object storage (blobs), hierarchical file systems, message queues, and event streams. This breadth means virtually any workload can find a managed service rather than needing to self-build from scratch.
Service Models: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS
Cloud services are grouped into three delivery models that differ in how much of the stack the provider manages versus how much the customer controls.
Model
Full Name
Provider Manages
Customer Manages
Azure Example
IaaS
Infrastructure as a Service
Physical servers, networking, virtualization
OS, runtime, middleware, application, data
Azure Virtual Machines
PaaS
Platform as a Service
Everything through the runtime
Application code and data
Azure App Service, Azure Functions
SaaS
Software as a Service
The entire stack including application
Configuration and user data
Microsoft 365, Azure DevOps

The higher in the stack the provider manages, the less operational overhead the customer carries — but also the less control over the underlying environment. IaaS gives the most flexibility; SaaS gives the least control but the least operational work. PaaS is the most popular model for new application development because it lets teams focus on code while the platform handles OS patching, runtime upgrades, and horizontal scaling.
Deployment Models
Beyond the service model, cloud computing is also categorized by who can access the resources and where they run.
Public cloud.

Resources run on shared infrastructure owned by the cloud provider and accessed over the internet. This is the default model — Azure's commercial offerings are public cloud. Any organization can provision resources, and the provider is responsible for physical security and hardware maintenance.
Private cloud.

Resources are provisioned exclusively for one organization, either in that organization's own data center (on-premises private cloud) or hosted by a provider as a dedicated environment. Azure Stack Hub allows organizations to run Azure services in their own data centers, keeping data fully on-premises while using familiar Azure APIs and tooling.
Hybrid cloud.

A combination of public cloud and private infrastructure connected by a network link (Azure ExpressRoute or VPN). Sensitive workloads or data that cannot leave the premises run on-site; everything else uses the public cloud. This is the most common enterprise model today.
Multi-cloud.

Using services from multiple public cloud providers simultaneously — for example, running primary workloads on Azure while using a competitor's machine learning platform. Multi-cloud improves negotiating power and avoids single-provider dependency but increases operational complexity.
When to Use the Cloud
Good Fit Scenarios
Applications with variable or unpredictable traffic that need to scale rapidly.
New products or startups that need to launch quickly without upfront hardware investment.
Workloads that require global presence across multiple geographic regions.
Applications that benefit from managed AI, ML, or analytics services that would be expensive to self-host.
Development and test environments that only run during business hours — cloud resources can be shut down overnight, cutting costs by 70%.
Disaster recovery and business continuity — replicating to a cloud region is far cheaper than building a standby data center.
When Not to Move to the Cloud
Ultra-latency-sensitive workloads (sub-millisecond) where network round-trips to the cloud are unacceptable — these belong on-premises or at the edge.
Workloads with strict data sovereignty requirements in jurisdictions without a compliant cloud region.
Legacy systems so tightly coupled to specific hardware that virtualization is not feasible without a complete rewrite.
Steady, large, CPU-bound workloads where three-year reserved capacity pricing would still exceed on-premises cost — financial modeling is essential before committing.
Getting Started with Azure

Azure provides three primary ways to create and manage resources: the Azure Portal (a web-based graphical interface), the Azure CLI (a cross-platform command-line tool), and Bicep/ARM templates (declarative infrastructure-as-code). All three ultimately call the Azure Resource Manager (ARM) API.

The fastest path to a working resource is the Azure CLI. The following commands create a resource group and deploy a managed web application entirely from the terminal, with no portal interaction required.

# Install the Azure CLI (macOS example; Linux/Windows also supported)
brew install azure-cli

# Log in to your Azure account — opens browser for interactive auth
az login

# Create a resource group (logical container for related resources)
az group create \
  --name myapp-rg \
  --location eastus

# Create an App Service plan (defines the compute tier)
az appservice plan create \
  --name myapp-plan \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --sku B1 \
  --is-linux

# Create a web app using the Python 3.11 runtime
az webapp create \
  --name my-unique-app-name-2025 \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --plan myapp-plan \
  --runtime "PYTHON:3.11"

# Retrieve the app's public URL
az webapp show \
  --name my-unique-app-name-2025 \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --query defaultHostName \
  --output tsv
# → my-unique-app-name-2025.azurewebsites.net
Every Azure resource belongs to a
resource group

— a logical container that groups related resources for management, billing, and access control. Deleting a resource group deletes everything inside it, which makes cleanup of demo or test environments a single command.
Examples
Provisioning a Virtual Machine
A virtual machine is the most fundamental IaaS resource. The CLI command below creates a Linux VM with SSH access pre-configured.

# Create a Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 LTS) with SSH key auth
az vm create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name my-linux-vm \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_B2s \   # 2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM — general purpose
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys \
  --output table

# Open port 80 for inbound HTTP traffic
az vm open-port \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name my-linux-vm \
  --port 80

# SSH into the VM using the auto-generated key
ssh azureuser@<PublicIpAddress>

# Clean up when done (stops billing immediately)
az group delete --name myapp-rg --yes --no-wait
Deploying a Serverless Function

Azure Functions is a PaaS compute service that runs code in response to events — HTTP requests, queue messages, timer schedules — without managing servers. The Consumption plan charges only when the function executes.

# Create a storage account (Functions require one for state)
az storage account create \
  --name mystorageacct2025 \
  --location eastus \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --sku Standard_LRS

# Create the Function App (Consumption plan = pay-per-execution)
az functionapp create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --consumption-plan-location eastus \
  --runtime python \
  --runtime-version 3.11 \
  --functions-version 4 \
  --name my-func-app-2025 \
  --storage-account mystorageacct2025 \
  --os-type linux
Real-World Example

A mid-sized e-commerce company with 500,000 daily active users migrated from a colocation data center to Azure over six months. The on-premises setup comprised 40 physical servers: 12 running the web tier, 8 running application servers, 4 running database primaries and replicas, and 16 for caching, search, and internal tools. The hardware was six years old, approaching end-of-support, and a hardware refresh would cost $800,000 capital expenditure.
On Azure, the architecture became:
Web tier:
Azure App Service with auto-scaling rules — 2 instances at night, up to 20 during peak hours. Cost: $0 when idle.
Application logic:
Azure Functions for background processing (order confirmations, inventory updates, email dispatch). Cost: per-invocation, effectively zero at low traffic.
Database:
Azure Database for PostgreSQL — Flexible Server with read replicas in a second region. Automatic backups and point-in-time restore replaced a manual backup script.
Cache:
Azure Cache for Redis in the Premium tier, with data persistence enabled.
Search:
Azure AI Search (formerly Cognitive Search) replaced an on-premises Elasticsearch cluster.
CDN:
Azure Front Door served static assets and cached API responses at 200+ edge locations globally, reducing origin load by 60%.

Total Azure monthly cost at steady state: approximately $9,200 — equivalent to $110,400 annually, compared to the $800,000 hardware refresh plus ongoing data-center power, cooling, and staff costs. More importantly, the engineering team was freed from hardware procurement cycles and OS patch management, and the time to provision a new environment for a feature branch dropped from three weeks to eight minutes.
Common Mistakes
Lift-and-shift without re-architecting

Moving a monolithic application directly to a cloud VM (a simple lift-and-shift) fails to capture most cloud benefits. The application still behaves as if it is on a single server: it cannot scale horizontally, it pays for idle compute, and it misses managed services that would eliminate operational work. A better approach is to modernize during migration — extract stateless services as functions or containers, move data to managed database services, and add auto-scaling policies.
Ignoring egress costs

Data ingest into Azure is free. Data leaving Azure (egress) is charged per gigabyte, with rates varying by region and destination. Applications that process large data sets inside the cloud and then export results or stream data to on-premises analytics tools often receive unexpected bills. Design data pipelines so heavy processing and transformation happen inside the cloud; minimize the bytes crossing the egress boundary.
Using a single region without redundancy

A cloud resource group in a single region is not inherently highly available. A misconfigured deployment, a data center failure, or a regional outage can take the service down. Production workloads should use at least two Availability Zones within a region for availability, and a secondary region for disaster recovery. Azure Traffic Manager or Azure Front Door route users to the nearest healthy endpoint automatically.
Leaving development resources running

Cloud resources accrue costs whenever they are running, not only when they are serving traffic. Developers frequently spin up VMs or databases to test a feature and forget to shut them down. Budget alerts, auto-shutdown policies on VMs, and tagging conventions (with automated cleanup jobs) are essential hygiene. A single forgotten GPU VM can cost thousands of dollars per month.
Best Practices
Tag every resource.

Tags are key-value metadata attached to Azure resources. Use tags for environment (dev/staging/prod), cost center, owner, and application name. Cost reports filtered by tag reveal where money is actually spent.
Use managed identities instead of credentials.

Azure Managed Identities give an application a machine-level identity without storing credentials in code or configuration files. The application obtains short-lived tokens from the Azure metadata service at runtime — no secrets to rotate or accidentally leak.
Apply the principle of least privilege.
Assign Azure RBAC roles at the narrowest scope possible (resource level, not subscription level). A web application that only reads from a storage container should have the
Storage Blob Data Reader
role on that specific container, not
Owner
on the subscription.
Enable diagnostic logs and Azure Monitor.

Every Azure service emits logs, metrics, and traces to Azure Monitor. Configure diagnostic settings to route data to a Log Analytics workspace. Set up alerts for critical thresholds (CPU > 90%, error rate > 1%, latency > 2s) before a problem appears in production.
Use infrastructure as code.

Define every resource in Bicep or Terraform. This makes environments reproducible, reviewable in version control, and prevents drift between environments. Never provision production resources manually in the portal if you can avoid it.
Choose the right service tier with Reserved Instances.

For workloads that run continuously, purchasing Reserved Instances or Savings Plans (1-year or 3-year commitments) can reduce compute cost by 40–72% compared to pay-as-you-go. Run the Azure Cost Management advisor monthly to identify reservation opportunities.
Performance and Scalability

Cloud-native applications are designed to scale horizontally — adding more instances of a service rather than making a single instance larger. Azure App Service, Azure Kubernetes Service, and Azure Container Apps all support rule-based or metric-driven auto-scaling: when average CPU exceeds 70% for five minutes, add two instances; when it drops below 30%, remove them.

For database performance, Azure SQL Database scales independently of the application tier. The DTU-based model bundles CPU, memory, and I/O into a single metric; the vCore model allows independent scaling of CPU and memory. Read scale-out directs read queries to secondary replicas, reducing load on the primary without application code changes.

Azure CDN and Azure Front Door cache static content and dynamic API responses at edge locations worldwide. For a global user base, serving content from an edge node 10 ms away rather than an origin server 200 ms away is often the single highest-impact performance improvement. Front Door also performs SSL termination at the edge, reducing the TCP handshake cost for every connection.

For compute-bound workloads, GPU and FPGA virtual machines are available in minutes, making it economical to run large-scale ML training jobs or financial risk calculations that would require months of capital planning on-premises. Spot instances (Azure Spot VMs) offer up to 90% discount for fault-tolerant, interruptible workloads.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Azure Portal:
The web-based graphical interface for managing all Azure resources. Best for exploration, monitoring dashboards, and one-off configuration tasks.
Azure CLI (
az
A cross-platform command-line tool for scripting and automation. Supports bash and PowerShell. Ideal for CI/CD pipelines and developer workflows.
Azure PowerShell (
Az
module):
PowerShell module for managing Azure resources. Preferred by Windows administrators and teams already invested in PowerShell automation.
Azure SDKs:
Official client libraries for Python, .NET, Java, JavaScript/TypeScript, Go, and others. Used to interact with Azure services programmatically from application code.
Bicep:

A domain-specific language (DSL) for Azure infrastructure as code. Bicep files compile to ARM templates. Cleaner syntax, type safety, and IDE support make it the recommended IaC approach for Azure-native deployments.
Terraform (AzureRM provider):
A provider-agnostic IaC tool with strong community support. Preferred when the same tool must manage Azure and other cloud providers simultaneously.
Azure DevOps and GitHub Actions:
CI/CD platforms with native Azure integration. Both offer pre-built actions/tasks for deploying to App Service, AKS, Functions, and static web apps.
Azure Cost Management + Billing:
A first-party tool for analyzing spending, setting budgets, and generating cost allocation reports by resource group, tag, or subscription.

Scalability

None
Low (managed services)
Elastic, global
Variable workloads, rapid iteration, SaaS products
On-Premises
High (hardware)
High (staff, maintenance)
Limited by hardware purchased
Ultra-sensitive latency, strict data sovereignty, large stable workloads
Colocation (Colo)
Medium (own hardware, rent space)
Medium (own software stack)
Manual, slow
Regulated industries that must own hardware but cannot manage a data center
Hybrid Cloud
Medium
Medium
Cloud-elastic for variable load, fixed on-premises for baseline
Enterprises with legacy systems or data-sovereignty requirements
Multi-Cloud
None
High (multiple toolchains)
Per-provider elastic
Avoiding single-vendor dependency, best-of-breed service selection
Security and Reliability

Cloud security operates on the shared responsibility model: the cloud provider secures the physical infrastructure, hypervisor, and managed service internals; the customer is responsible for identity, access control, data classification, and application security. Understanding exactly where provider responsibility ends and customer responsibility begins is a foundational requirement for any cloud deployment.

Azure provides several layers of default protection. All data at rest is encrypted by default using AES-256; Azure Key Vault manages encryption keys with hardware security modules (HSMs) and supports customer-managed keys for regulated workloads. Data in transit is encrypted using TLS 1.2 or higher. Azure DDoS Protection Basic is enabled by default on all public endpoints, with an upgradeable Standard tier providing adaptive tuning and attack analytics.

Identity is the new perimeter in cloud environments. Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) provides identity and access management: multi-factor authentication, conditional access policies, privileged identity management (just-in-time admin access), and identity protection that detects anomalous sign-in patterns using machine learning.

Reliability is engineered through redundancy at every layer. Azure publishes Service Level Agreements for each service: Azure Storage guarantees 99.9% to 99.99% read availability depending on the redundancy tier selected. For highest availability, mission-critical applications should be deployed across multiple availability zones (within a region) and multiple regions (for disaster recovery), with Azure Traffic Manager or Azure Front Door directing traffic to the nearest healthy endpoint.
Mental Model

Think of the cloud as a utility — similar to electricity. Before electric grids existed, every factory had to build and operate its own generator: capital expenditure upfront, ongoing fuel and maintenance costs, and engineers dedicated to keeping the generator running. Once the electric grid appeared, factories could simply plug in and pay for the kilowatt-hours they consumed. They still controlled what machines ran and what products they made; they just no longer needed to be in the power-generation business.

The cloud is computing-as-a-utility. Organizations still control their applications, data models, and business logic — but they are no longer in the data-center-operations business unless they choose to be. The cloud provider manages the power, cooling, hardware refresh, hypervisor, and increasingly the operating system and runtime. Customers consume CPU cycles, gigabytes of storage, and API calls the same way they consume kilowatt-hours: on demand, metered, and billed for actual consumption.

The analogy also clarifies the trade-off. A factory that builds its own generator has more control over power quality and zero dependency on the grid — but at far higher cost and operational complexity. Some organizations, for regulatory or latency reasons, still make that choice. For most workloads, plugging into the grid is the right decision.
Key Takeaways

The cloud is a global network of data centers that delivers compute, storage, database, networking, AI, and security services over the internet, billed by consumption rather than by hardware ownership.
The five essential NIST characteristics of cloud computing are: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service.
Service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — describe how much of the stack the provider manages. PaaS and SaaS reduce operational overhead; IaaS provides the most control.
Deployment models — public, private, hybrid, and multi-cloud — describe who can access the resources and where they run. Hybrid cloud is the dominant enterprise pattern today.

The primary benefits are elasticity, pay-as-you-go pricing, global reach, access to managed services, and reduced time to market. The primary trade-offs are ongoing cost for steady workloads, vendor lock-in, and egress charges.
Azure CLI, Bicep, and Terraform enable infrastructure-as-code: environments are version-controlled, reproducible, and auditable — never configured manually in ways that can't be replicated.
Security is a shared responsibility. The provider secures the physical and hypervisor layers; the customer is responsible for identity, access control, application code, and data classification.

The mental model is utility computing: as electricity grids freed factories from running their own generators, the cloud frees organizations from running their own data centers — allowing focus on the application rather than the infrastructure beneath it.
